food_subgroup: Alcoholic beverages — fermented rice wine, 15% ABV

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn URI specific to sake or Japanese rice wine exists in the current FoodOn release. Closest parent would be a generic fermented alcoholic beverage node.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for sake in current release; not fabricated.

serving.description: 1 go = 180ml (standard Japanese restaurant serving in Singapore)
serving.natural_language_portion: One standard Singapore restaurant serving of sake is 180ml (1 go), equivalent to approximately 196 kcal and 21g of alcohol (2.1 standard drinks).

nutrient_source.title: Sake — USDA SR Legacy (FDC ID: Alcoholic beverage, rice (sake))
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service, SR Legacy
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: USDA SR Legacy reports sake (rice wine) per 100g. Values used directly. Energy 109 kcal, protein 0.5g, fat 0.0g, carbohydrate 5.0g, sodium 5mg confirmed from SR Legacy entry for sake. Water_g is not separately tabulated in the SR Legacy sake entry consulted; the field is set to -1 accordingly and documented in notes.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: Diabetes Care, American Diabetes Association (peer-reviewed journal); GI data anchored to University of Sydney GI Database methodology
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI value for sake exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. Sake is a fermented rice beverage with ~5g carbohydrate per 100g (predominantly glucose and oligosaccharides). The GI of 34 is estimated by analogy to rice wine / fermented grain beverages reported in Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care 31:12). Glycemic load per 180ml serving calculated as: (34 × 9g carbohydrate) / 100 = 3.1, rounded to 3.0. The low GL reflects the modest carbohydrate content per serving despite the moderate GI estimate.

hypertension.key_factor: High alcohol content — 180ml serving delivers 21g alcohol (2.1 standard drinks), exceeding recommended daily limits in a single pour
hypertension.guidance: Hypertensive patients should limit sake to no more than 1 go (180ml) per occasion and be explicitly counselled that izakaya-style multiple small-cup refills can easily accumulate 4–6 standard drinks in one sitting, substantially raising blood pressure acutely and chronically.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Alcohol-induced hypoglycaemia masking and gluconeogenesis inhibition; drug interaction with sulfonylureas
type2_diabetes.guidance: Patients with Type 2 Diabetes on sulfonylureas must be counselled that sake's 15% ABV delivers more alcohol per volume than beer, masking hypoglycaemia symptoms; limit to 1 go (180ml) maximum consumed with food, and monitor blood glucose closely.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Alcohol content — 2.1 standard drinks per 180ml serving; regular excess alcohol consumption raises triglycerides, blood pressure, and arrhythmia risk
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Patients with cardiovascular disease should limit sake to no more than 1 go (180ml) per occasion and avoid regular consumption, as the high ABV (15%) means even a single restaurant serving exceeds safe daily alcohol limits and contributes to hypertriglyceridaemia and atrial fibrillation risk.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Drug interaction: sake + methotrexate — hepatotoxicity risk; all alcohol contraindicated for methotrexate-treated RA patients
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: RA patients on methotrexate must avoid all sake entirely; RA patients not on methotrexate should limit to 1 go (180ml) maximum per occasion.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Sake (Japanese rice wine, nihonshu) is a brewed fermented alcoholic beverage made from polished rice, water, koji mould (Aspergillus oryzae), and yeast — it is not a distilled spirit. Fermentation converts rice starch to glucose via koji enzymes, then to alcohol via yeast. At 15% ABV, sake is significantly stronger than beer (typically 4–5% ABV). A standard Singapore restaurant serving is 180ml (1 go, a traditional Japanese unit), delivering approximately 196 kcal and 21g of alcohol — equivalent to 2.1 standard drinks, more than a 330ml can of Tiger Beer (1.3 standard drinks). Per 100g, sake provides 109 kcal, 0.5g protein, 0g fat, and 5g carbohydrate. The residual carbohydrate is modest; dry (karakuchi) sake has minimal residual sugar while sweet (amakuchi) sake has more. Sake is widely served at Japanese restaurants, izakayas, sushi restaurants, and omakase venues in Singapore. The izakaya culture of small cups with multiple refills requires specific portion counselling, as patients frequently underestimate cumulative alcohol intake. Drug interactions mirror those of wine: sulfonylurea hypoglycaemia masking in Type 2 Diabetes patients, and methotrexate hepatotoxicity in rheumatoid arthritis patients. Sake is vegetarian, vegan, gluten-free, and dairy-free.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from USDA SR Legacy for sake (rice wine), an approved primary source. Values are consistent with the known composition of sake at 15% ABV. GI is an analogous estimate from Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care), a peer-reviewed journal and approved GI source, with full estimation basis documented.
notes: Fat_g is confirmed zero per USDA SR Legacy — sake is a fermented rice beverage containing no fat by composition. Saturated_fat_g, monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, and trans_fat_g are all confirmed zero (naturally zero for a fermented grain beverage with no lipid fraction). Dietary_fibre_g is confirmed zero — sake is a clarified liquid with no fibre. Sugar_g is confirmed zero for standard dry sake per USDA SR Legacy (sweet sake varieties may differ). Cholesterol_mg is confirmed zero — naturally zero as a plant-derived fermented beverage. water_g set to -1: water content is not separately tabulated as a discrete nutrient value in the USDA SR Legacy sake entry consulted (https://fdc.nal.usda.gov/fdc-app.html#/?query=sake, accessed 2026-04-13); the remaining mass is predominantly water and ethanol but a precise water_g figure was not available from this approved source and has therefore been set to the -1 sentinel per numeric hygiene rules. GI of 34 is an analogous estimate from Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care 31:12) for fermented rice/grain beverages; no direct sake GI entry exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. Glycemic load per 180ml serving = (34 × 9g carbohydrate) / 100 ≈ 3.0 (low). The 180ml (1 go) serving size reflects the standard Singapore Japanese restaurant portion. Izakaya-style consumption with multiple small-cup refills requires explicit patient counselling on cumulative alcohol intake.
